advantages: Decomposes complex derivatives into simple local factors—each layer or primitive only needs its own Jacobian or scalar derivative.
Makes deep network training tractable: reverse-mode chain rule (backprop) shares work across parameters efficiently.
Powers automatic differentiation—write forward code once; frameworks chain rules correctly at scale.
Unifies calculus and ML intuition: sensitivity through a pipeline is a product (or sum of products) of local sensitivities.
disadvantages: Deep chains multiply many factors, causing vanishing or exploding gradients without careful activations, normalization or residual paths.
Non-smooth ops (ReLU, max-pool) need subgradient conventions; naive chain rule at kinks can confuse analysis.
Manual chain-rule derivations for large graphs are error-prone—bugs silently produce wrong training dynamics.
Reverse-mode stores intermediate activations for the backward pass, increasing memory cost versus forward-only evaluation.
